item.title: Shadowheart elegant robe cosplay by me
item.dc:creator: scarlettsirene

item.title: Rustic Dublin Coddle with Sausage and Potatoes
item.description: Needs a French bread loaf. Rustic Dublin Coddle with Sausage and Potatoes Ingredients: 4 large potatoes, peeled and sliced 1 pound pork sausages 8 slices of bacon, chopped (I’m using pork jowl for the first time) 2 large onions, sliced 2 cloves of garlic, minced 2 cups chicken broth 1 cup water 1 teaspoon dried parsley 1 teaspoon dried thyme Salt and pepper to taste Directions: 1. Preheat your oven to 350°F (175°C). 2. In a large skillet, cook the bacon over medium heat until crisp. Remove bacon and set aside, leaving the fat in the skillet. 3. Brown the sausages in the bacon fat, then set aside with the bacon. 4. In the same skillet, sauté onions and garlic until translucent. 5. Layer half of the potatoes in a large baking dish, followed by half of the onions, garlic, bacon, and sausages. Repeat the layers with the remaining ingredients. 6. Pour chicken broth and water over the layers and sprinkle with dried parsley, thyme, salt, and pepper. 7. Cover the dish with foil and bake in the preheated oven for about 1 hour, or until the potatoes are tender.
item.dc:creator: Joyika

.

item.title: In The Lord of the Rings, the filmmakers used a special lighting rig for Galadriel so that her eyes appear to reflect the starlight. This is because Galadriel is the last Elf in Middle-Earth to have seen the light of the Trees of Valinor.

item.dc:creator: MetaPathos

item.title: Let's Make Crispy Roast Potatoes
item.description:
Hey hey potato fans! Today I made crispy roast potatoes, using the baking soda method. We are gonna make the most delicious, golden-and-crunchy-on-the-outside, soft-and-fluffy-on-the-inside, oven roasted potatoes EVER using the magic of science! SCIENCE! I like these because you can make them using minimal ingredients (potatoes, water, salt, oil, baking soda) or fancy them up to your taste.
First, we're going to need some potatoes. I'm using reds today because it's what I had. Russets will give you the crispiest outsides but roast up kind of pale. Reds don't get as crisp on the outside but brown up oh so nicely. Yukon gold is going to be your sweet middle ground. Use those if you can! I used 9 medium sized potatoes here and that was too many. I always over estimate how many potatoes I'm going to need. 6-7 of these would have been sufficient.
Other notables: - Baking Soda: you will definitely need baking soda, 1/2 teaspoon will do. You might think you'll need more than that, trust me, you don't. - Seasoning: I tried this Cajun blend today and it turned out quite well. You could also go with salt and pepper if you want to keep things simple. The garlic I used to infuse my oil (not pictured here). Be aware of what you add in the seasoning stage because it will burn and may turn bitter. - Oil: I had leftover duck fat so that's what I went with. You could also use canola oil, corn oil, bacon fat... whatever can handle high heat. You'll need about 5 tablespoons of oil.
Chop them potatoes into large chunks. If you cut them too small they will disintegrate into sludge and you will be sad. Slicing into wedges also works rather well. I leave the skins on because I find it appealing. If you want them to come out consistently golden all around, take 'em to the peelers. You do you!
"Double, double toil and trouble; Fire burn and caldron bubble." Bring a large pot of salted water to a boil, add your half tsp. of baking soda, and then gently add them taters. Once it's back up to a rolling boil again, set a 5 minute timer. We want to parboil them, not cook them through. (The *sizzle sizzle* is from the garlic I had going on the next burner)
Here I am infusing my duck fat with some crushed garlic cloves. Apparently I used 8 but use as much or as little as you like. If I had it, I would have also added rosemary in this step. But I didn't, hence the Cajun spice.
Weird angle shot of the garlic removed from the infused oil. Hold onto those golden nuggets of flavor, we aren't done with them yet!
Once your potatoes are done boiling, remove them from the water to a large bowl. Add a couple tablespoons of your oil and start tossing, adding more oil as you go. They should get to a point where you can't toss them anymore because they'll all be glued to each other. We want this! That starchy coating is what's going to make them delicious! Add your seasoning (salt, pepper, spice mix) and use a rubber spatula or wooden spoon to get them evenly coated.
Pre-heat your oven to either 450°F (232°C) if you're using a standard oven, or 400°F (205°C) if you're using a convection oven. The circulating air helps them cook more evenly which equals more goodness. My oven automatically takes off 25°F when using the convection feature so I had to trick it and set it to 425°F. Annoying. Spread them soon-to-be-tasty nuggets onto a baking sheet. This is also the point where I realized I had chopped up too many potatoes. This is a prime example of pan overcrowding. Look at this glorious mistake! Ideally you want a bit of room between each one. Pop them into the oven and set a timer for 20 minutes because that's when we'll need to flip them.
While that was going on, I minced up the cooked garlic, cleaned up some dishes, and drank some tea. Clean as you go. You'll thank yourself later.
When the 20 minutes is up, CAREFULLY remove the sheet from the oven and flip each one over as best you can. By carefully I mean allow the steam to escape before you stick your arms in there. I was once waiting in a hospital emergency room and there was another woman there who had severe burns up both arms because she didn't wait for the steam to escape before pulling out her turkey. Big ouch! :< Put them back in and set a 30 minute timer. Feel free to check them every 10-15 minutes to shake or flip them after this point. When there was only a couple minutes left, I added my garlic back in and gave them a bit of salt because they needed it.
And that's it! I sprinkled on some dried parsley because I was feeling fancy af. These work well for breakfast, second breakfast, brunch, lunch, supper... you get the idea. I'm eating them on their own because that's how I roll. Oh potatoes, you'll always be the main show for me. I would never try to make you my Camilla, ever! You shouldn't let anyone make you their Camilla either, unless it's what you want. Don't settle for being second best, you are a star, baby. A star!
Cat tax~ He was very curious about what I was eating so of course he had to smell check. Most viral edit: y'all are wild! Thanks for loving potatoes as much as I do. <3
item.dc:creator: andpotatoesforall

item.title: Some Programming Humor For A Niche Audience (BLESSED I.T. SUNDAY)
item.description:
1_WhatIsThisCreature
2_WhenYouTellAsianDadThatYouWIllNotBeAProgammer
3_WriteThisDown
4_GoodThingTheyWrappedTheCablesInNiceCircularObjectsOtherwiseItCouldHaveBeenAMess
5
6_FoundInTheWild
7_WebsitesTheseDays
8_LeastToxicProductManager
9
10_WhatIsHolePics
11
12_GuysWeShouldStartOurOwnTechStartUp
13_ITSupportInOtherSItuations_part_1
14_ITSupportInOtherSItuations_part_2
15. Thank you for making it all the way down! I feel like writing today and would like to share a little bit about a passion project I'm working on and why this means so much to me. Do Not Press The Button is more than just a game to me—it's a labor of love, born out of countless hours of passion, creativity, and dedication. When I first started working on this project, my goal was to create something that would not only entertain but also resonate with players on a deeper level. It started as a goofy idea, but now, almost at the finish line, I believe I've managed to give it heart, soul, and spirit. As an independent developer, the journey has been both incredibly rewarding and challenging. I've poured everything I've got into this project, and now, I'm at a point where I need your support to bring this dream fully to life. I believe in Do Not Press The Button with all my heart, and I truly hope it will bring as much joy to you as it has brought me while creating it. Your support—whether through wishlisting, sharing, or simply cheering from the sidelines—would mean the world to me. Thank you for considering this and for being part of this journey. I couldn’t do this without you. https://store.steampowered.com/app/2336120/Do_Not_Press_The_Button_To_Delete_The_Multiverse/ P.S. Have an amazing SUNDAY!
item.dc:creator: TheseusGames

item.title: Yet Another National Cash Register Restoration Begins! (Slimy Edition)
item.description:
Well alright then, it's been a minute since we've done one of these! For the uninitiated, hi and welcome to the hobby! Above is a 1906 brass National Cash Register that I bought from a gentleman not too far from where I live who had been trying unsuccessfully to sell it for the past year. I had been eyeballing it for most of that time, but because it was listed at such a high price I'd been giving it a pass. After finishing my most recent restoration I decided to finally send the seller a message and ask if he'd be willing to consider coming down to a more reasonable price. After some surprisingly polite haggling, we settled on $200 instead of the $700 he'd been asking. The caveat was that this register was something of an unwanted family heirloom that his great grandmother had used in her dairy store back when the seller was a kid. He'd gotten saddled with it, but he had memories of playing with it when it was in better condition and wants me to send photos that he can frame when I'm done. I think that's more than fair, which means I'm going to be putting a little more elbow grease into this project than previous ones. (If you'd like to help support these restorations, I accept small donations to put toward replacement parts and cleaning supplies: https://ko-fi.com/elbowdeepinahorse) Now... onto the *problems.*
So. If you've been looking closely, you've probably noticed all of the *green* smeared everywhere on the brass chassis. If you remember from previous projects, you'll know that green mystery substances tend to be a bad thing when they show up on brass plating. Usually because something is oxidizing said plating. After wiping some of the green death off, I'm reasonably certain that it isn't due to the brass being eaten alive. It's from what the original owner did to "clean" it. So yeah. According to him, in order to keep the register from seizing up, he opened up the case and sprayed a shitload of Brakleen inside and used WD-40 to clean the outside. Just gobbed it on, wiped it around, and called it square because I'm sure at the time it looked very shiny. The problem is, after sitting in storage for how many years, all that solvent and cleaner has since turned into green chemical goo. This is the slimiest cash register I've ever touched. It's... kind of awful.
The first thing I thought when I saw the receipt box, and saw that it didn't latch properly, is that oh god he sprayed all that stuff into the printing mechanism too. I mean, the green goblin is all over it.
Eeyup. He did. For context, some of those rollers are - were - plated nickel. I haven't been brave enough to investigate whether that plating is still there or if it reacted with the chemical romance he used to shine it all up. But, yeah, you can see how all that green is puddled up beneath where the door closes. It's not great. It's actually kind of really bad. There's a 50% chance that there's any brass left under that oxidation layer. Hooboy.
As for the price flags, it's a pretty standard sight for mistreated registers. The paint has long since begun to flake away and the spring steel has started to catch a case of the crusty rusties. This might actually be a case where the cleaner actually helped a little, but so many of the decals have all but melted off that it's nothing to write home about. They're going to have to be removed, soaked in Evaporust, repainted, and have new decals put on. In total, it's going to run around $75 to buy a new set of decals and UV resistant paint/primer.
The front of the chassis is pretty clear of Hulk skeet, which tells me that's probably what got wiped down the most after the green clean. Weirdly enough, I've never seen a register with brass framing to hold the glass panes in. It's hard to see, but you can tell where it's gotten bent over time because as far as I can tell, it *is* 100% brass for that part. Should be interesting trying to straighten that out, though I think I'll end up leaving it if I can avoid it. As for the dollar and cent keys, hooboy. Some can probably be salvaged, and obviously some need to be replaced. Either way, I'll need to buy a full set of numbered caps and hope I have enough spare rings downstairs to fix the ones that are cracked. So, add another $25 to the repair bill. :)
A better look at the key rings. Some of them are missing backers, some have popped loose because they've cracked like the one on the shelf, and the list goes on. I'll likely need to replace all of them so they match, but we'll see. I might try to save the originals, but the visual difference between new and old can be pretty jarring. The guy I get new ones from stamps them out of plastic, and the originals are glass ovals that got etched and painted on the underside. Bit of a difference. As for the marble shelf, I'm fifty fifty. The break has been epoxied back together but it's collected a lot of grime and gunk. The corners are beaten up, but they're not razor sharp. I'm going to try to clean up that break and reseal it - something I haven't done a lot of if I'm being honest - and wet sand the chipped corners to see if I can't smooth them out.
By some miracle, all that chemical bath doesn't appear to have gotten into the mahogany drawer. At this point, I'll take what I can get for good news! :D
The back panel, because holy moly this register is more green and gold than Green Bay.
*shudder* I mean, what is there to say? There's just so much of this crap everywhere, and every surface of the register is like touching melted butter. It got everywhere!
As for these counters... there are more numbers than I expected to find. No labels for them either. This is a mystery I'm going to have to solve later, but I'm pretty sure NCR didn't expect money to be tracked up to a million dollars and down to the hundredth of a cent. ;) Also, missing a knob up there. Hecking heck. Gotta find one of those too.
Close-up on the medallions they used as bolt washers. I dunno, I thought this was pretty cool. 500% chance one of these flies off at orbital velocity when I put them under the buffing wheel.
The keys. The cracked rings. The bit of cardboard that they glued down to replace a busted glass cap. Auauugh...
Also missing a bell lock. Ironically, I have a replacement bell lock for this machine! Huzzah!
Okay, I think I took more photos than I realized. Last patent date for this register was in 1896, however the serial number dates to 1906. Man, those were the days.
Model 39: Green Edition! This is the goo that came off that 39 medallion. JUST that medallion. ANYHOO, enough whining. Time to disassemble this slimeball!
First up, taking off the endcaps that hold the glass in place. Bippidy boppidy poof!
Next come those fragile brass frames that hold the bottom glass. Oh, speaking of glass. It's going to cost me $90 to order custom beveled glass for this bugger. Happy thoughts. Happy thoughts...
Off comes the lid and the decorative breastplate. That green goo... well it's green goo. All the stuff that got trapped between the lid and breastplate for how many years. It's like poster putty!
Yummy yum! Shame I won't be able to retain this deep brass coloring, though. With all the schmoo that's been coated over everything, it needs a deep clean and that's going to affect the color. No sense in going halfway. This thing's getting extra time under the polishing wheel.
THE GOO OF SAURON SEES YOU
Taking a look under the hood, I'm seeing some nominal spots where rust has started to form in spite of or because of all the chems that were sprayed in here. It's not bad, but it's not great either.
The panel pile grows. Also, I'm beginning to notice that a lot of the original screws are sort of... gone.
Not these guys, though. Gonna pretty these original uglies up.
TREASURE. Heck yeah, now I've got an AutoZone discount thingy and a state quarter! And a paperclip! I'm rich!
Waiiiit a minute. A counterfeit pen! More treasure!
Taking a top-down for posterity when I finally have to reinstall the flags. Screw up this pattern and you'll have a jammed register faster than you can blink. Also you'll screw up the paint job on your new flags.
I mean, I will say this. There's no dust in there. Probably because the brake cleaner sent it straight to hell.
Taking the front plate off the drawer box, and it's not in too bad condition. Even still has the chalk lines from when the original workers labeled it on the assembly line.
More brass! *Home Improvement grunting*
Oh god it's even worse in the sunlight. Also, take a moment to realize that I didn't remove any screws yet. There's literally ONE holding the side panel on. Also, missing two more locks. Which I don't have replacements for at the moment. That's going to be a fun eBay hunt.
Look how strong this little guy is. Don't worry, I'll find you new friends.
Okay, I made a mistake and looked closer. Fuck me hard, that is a mess. I think it's safe to say that this printer box is chowdered. Worse, it's missing parts. What a freaking shame.
By Grabthar's Hammer, I will avenge you.
Sneaky internal bolt is sneaky.
Broken price flag is broken. Fuck. That might actually be a problem if I don't have a replacement in that specific orientation.
It's hard to see, but do you see the gap between the side panel and the bottom frame? That's because there are a total of NO screws holding them together. I only discovered that later during disassembly, but the only thing holding that base frame on is green grime. I do not know how this register survived this long. I am actually a little irritated at how it got taken apart by someone, then mushed back together without half of its fasteners.
At least the frame looks alright. Heck, even the cash box looks pretty good considering the hell it endured. The bell doesn't exactly ring, though. It's more accurate to say it tonks. More on that in another update.
Okay, here's where you can see the layers and layers of chemicals in evidence. Those brown tags hanging off the back are cast iron. They're meant to be the same color as the rest of the surrounding metal. There are ripples where each layer of gunk solidified over the previous. Have I mentioned that this register smells like a wet penny from across the room? Because it do. It do.
Whelp, here's the money shot. I'll be honest, it isn't a good start. I'll be replacing a lot of ruined parts, searching my donor machines downstairs for anything I can salvage, and I'm genuinely unsure of what the panels are going to look like once I wash the green off. It could be nothing. It could be a strip mine where the brass used to be. I guess we'll spin the wheel!
Whichever it is, I told the guy I bought it from that I'd get it cleaned up and looking nice so that's what I'm going to do. I think it's fair to say that it won't come out looking perfect but that's kind of what antiques are. This register has seen a lot of mileage, a lot of it on gravel roads, so we'll see what happens when it happens.
Until the next update, Imgur friends! As a reward for reaching the bottom, you've earned yourselves a dad joke!
Sauce: fizzlefer
item.dc:creator: ElbowDeepInAHorse

item.title: My mother forgot who I am, but I will never stop loving her
item.dc:creator: juninhofellas
